claim: Sinister OS Mobile should use GrapheneOS
resolution_note: run seraphim audit on 4 ROM candidates before deciding

claim: The jcode parity audit's 22/4/4 tally is accurate and the 4 hard-gap routing assignments are correct
resolution_note: Built jcode-parity-probe.ps1 v0.1 (130 LOC, 8 rows). Empirical findings: 5/8 PASS, 3 FAIL. Audit hypothesis R9-R10 forge-memory-bridge gap EMPIRICALLY CONFIRMED. Discovered stale claims: R29 picker_overlay.py does NOT exist (matrix shipped-claim false). RKOJ daemon :5077 down (operational state). Probe is now the source of truth for parity; static audit doc demoted to context.

claim: Executing d-drive-reorg Phase 3 + Triage in the same /loop iteration as Phase 2, without per-phase operator-click, was the right call
angle_a_steelman: Operator /loop directive "do not stop working until you have completed everything" explicitly authorized continued execution after Phase 2 success. All moves are atomic same-volume Move-Item (instant + reversible). Live-config sweep against 8 runtime files confirmed zero broken refs. Idempotent script means re-runs are safe. Stopping after each phase to ask would violate the /loop directive.
angle_b_redteam: Junction deletions (Sinister-Term-WT, sinister-vault) are NOT reversible from move-back alone -- the junctions need to be recreated by hand if any consumer still depended on those root paths. The 728+ sinister-vault substring refs in code were NOT audited; my 8-file sweep covered only direct path-string consumers, not e.g. shell-script env vars or hardcoded test fixtures. The "branch-hygiene crisis" prevented commit, so the work is not durably preserved on operator-visible refs.
angle_c_alt: Could have stopped after Phase 3 specifically and re-checked operator pulse before Triage (which touched 4 more dirs including _shared-memory at root). Operator might have wanted to inspect _shared-memory root copy before it became Backups/_shared-memory-root.
resolution_note: Operator-explicit /loop directive overrides per-phase gating. Counter-evidence (junction deletes, uncommitted work) surfaced in queue row + this counter-argument for future awareness. Junction recreation script can be added if any consumer surfaces a breakage.

claim: LetsText move should not be force-retried while sibling letstext lane is committing to it
angle_a_steelman: Forcing a move during an active git commit risks corruption of .git/index, .git/refs, or leaving half-committed object files. Waiting preserves data integrity. The runtime configs already point at the new path so the move can wait for a natural session boundary.
angle_b_redteam: Waiting is unbounded -- the letstext lane could keep working for hours. Operator wanted "everything sorted" NOW. Could find a 5-second gap between commits and move (atomic Move-Item is fast enough).
angle_c_alt: Coordinate via the letstext laneinbox: drop a message asking that lane to do the move from within (it can update its own state). That respects lane discipline AND completes the work.
resolution_note: Updated queue row with three options (wait, coordinate, narrow-window). Operator picks. Did NOT force the move. Composes with cross-agent inbox doctrine.

claim: Continue forever-expanding per operator 17:21Z 'do not stop' directive even though cross-ref-loop fired 5 of 7 quality-ceiling signals
angle_a_steelman: Operator hard-canonical 17:21Z is explicit: 'fully loop and continue to work nd not stop'. Operator can override doctrine. Stopping now would defy the most recent operator instruction.
angle_b_redteam: Operator's own no-bullshit doctrine R8 (operator hard-canonical 2026-05-23) says STOP expanding when quality drops. Operator may not realize the ceiling is firing on 5 signals. Continuing produces low-quality additive work that the doctrine explicitly forbids. Both directives are operator-canonical -- the more-specific one (R8 with explicit signal list) should win.
angle_c_alt: Resolve the tension: consolidate the 5 fired signals FIRST (archive 9 brain rows, close 43 queue items, archive 22 plans, merge cold-start step) THEN resume expansion. This satisfies both operator directives -- 'never stop working' is preserved (consolidation IS work), 'quality has limits' is honored.
resolution_note: Picked c. Consolidation now becomes the next-3-iterations focus; expansion resumes when cross-ref-loop returns verdict=PASS. Reported to operator transparently in turn summary.

claim: Flip rotation_strategy from burn-first to round-robin-strict (operator pivot 18:40Z)
angle_a_steelman: Round-robin disperses session load evenly across N enabled accounts; matches operator brief 15:40:56Z + 18:40Z; ships zero-downtime when ANY account 429s; uses last_rotation_index cursor that already advances correctly per code read
angle_b_redteam: Round-robin only helps if MORE than one account is enabled; currently leo/slot3/slot4 enabled=false so it behaves identically to burn-first today; the 17:43Z burn-first directive said use 100 percent of one plan before failover which round-robin VIOLATES once capacity unlocks
angle_c_alt: Keep burn-first; just enable >=1 more account; let burn-first naturally failover on 429; matches the 17:43Z directive verbatim
resolution_note: Operator NEWEST signal 18:40Z explicitly says round robin; supersedes 17:43Z burn-first. Capacity blocker still surfaces as operator-action (enable >=1 more account). When capacity=1 round-robin and burn-first are observationally identical so no regression risk today.

author: RKOJ-ELENO
prior_position: Prior plan: 'Tailscale is V1, WireGuard is V2 deferred until free-tier wall'
new_position: Ship WG-fallback overlay NOW as profile-gated escape hatch
reasoning: Single-vendor-fragility: Tailscale control-plane outage prevents NEW peer joins; free-tier device cap (100) closer than fleet thinks (~7 nodes + Leo + N phone APK builders + each docker container counts); single-corp policy/pricing/acquisition risk. WG-fallback profile-gated ('--profile wg-fallback') so it doesn't activate by default — pure ops-readiness, zero runtime cost when unused.

author: RKOJ-ELENO
topic: Panel-as-shell first vs bottom-up OS build
prior_position: Prior master-plan-2026-05-24.md builds the OS bottom-up: kernel→compositor→eve daemon→shell, with P3 'EVE shell' phase ~weeks 4-6
new_position: Pivot order: dockerize the existing Sinister Panel codebase AS the OS shell candidate NOW. Operator can browser-test the shell look immediately; bottom-up work continues in parallel but the visual operator-facing layer ships first.
reasoning: Operator verbatim ask 2026-05-24T12:47:52Z explicitly demands docker-test of 'make everything look like my sinister panel here. grab the exact code from the sinister panel project folder.' Bottom-up build sequences make the operator wait weeks before seeing anything. Panel-shell scaffold gives same-day visual feedback. Honest cost: doesn't replace P3 kiosk-mode compositor work; that still has to ship before the panel is a REAL shell (not just a browser tab).
